interviewStages: Behavioral: collaboration, writing skill, customer empathy for developer audiences
codingTopics: Columnar and streaming: high-cardinality event ingestion
Data structures: hash maps, heaps for top-K fields
Anomaly detection: statistical binning over fields
systemDesignTopics: Design a high-cardinality event store optimized for slice-and-dice queries
Design distributed tracing ingestion at 1M spans per second
Design BubbleUp-style automated anomaly detection across hundreds of fields
Design a columnar query engine for wide events with fast filters

coding: Given a stream of wide events, compute group-by aggregations on an arbitrary high-cardinality field over a time window. Discuss memory bounds.
Implement BubbleUp: given a slow set and a baseline set of events, rank the fields whose value distributions differ most. Discuss the approach.
Parse and ingest a stream of trace spans, reconstructing parent-child trees per trace ID. Discuss the data structure.
Maintain approximate top-K distinct values for a high-cardinality column as events stream in, with bounded memory.
behavioral: Honeycomb is writing-focused. Tell me about a time a written design or doc changed a decision.
Describe a time you deeply empathized with developer users debugging production.
Give an example of collaborating openly and sharing your work publicly.
Tell me about a time you debugged a genuinely hard, high-cardinality production issue.

Design distributed tracing ingestion that sustains 1M spans per second.
Design BubbleUp-style automated anomaly detection across hundreds of fields.
values: Honeycomb blogs openly about how they work. Describe a time you made your reasoning transparent to others.
How do you build for engineers debugging under pressure at 3am?
Tell me about a time you handled a mistake in a blameless, learning-focused way.

rawSummary:
Honeycomb Interview Guide (2026): Observability for Modern Systems
By 			
				
				techinterview.org			
			
		

		 /  May 5, 2026 
			
		

	
	
	

		
		
On this page

- Process

- What they actually ask

- Levels and comp (2026)

- Prep priorities

- Frequently Asked Questions

Updated May 5, 2026 · techinterview.org

⏱ 1 min read

Honeycomb
honeycomb.io ↗

Honeycomb is the technically respected observability-for-engineers platform — wide events, high-cardinality fields, BubbleUp anomaly detection. The engineering team is thoughtful, opinionated, and extremely public about what they do (Charity Majors' blog, the Honeycomb engineering blog, and SREcon talks). The interview is async-friendly, writing-focused, and rewards engineers who already engage with their content.

Process

Recruiter screen → take-home async work sample (paid, ~3 hours) → 60-minute pair-programming → 60-minute system design → 60-minute past-project deep dive → behavioral. Cycle: 3–5 weeks.

What they actually ask

- Design a high-cardinality event store optimized for slice-and-dice queries

- Design distributed tracing ingestion at 1M spans/sec

- Design BubbleUp — automated anomaly detection across hundreds of fields

- Coding: Go (the bulk of the codebase), often involving streaming or columnar processing

- Behavioral: collaboration, writing skill, customer empathy for developer audiences

Levels and comp (2026)

- SE II: $170K–$210K total

- Senior SE: $240K–$310K

- Staff: $340K–$440K

- Principal: $470K–$610K

Prep priorities

- Read the Honeycomb blog and OpenTelemetry docs — Honeycomb employees lead OTel

- Be fluent in Go and at least one observability stack (Prometheus, Grafana, Tempo)

- Understand columnar storage, time-series compression, and trace data models

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Honeycomb fully remote?

Yes. Distributed across US and Canada with some EU presence. ~250 employees.

How does Honeycomb compare to Datadog?

Datadog is broader (infra + APM + logs). Honeycomb is deeper on developer-debugging via wide events. Smaller team, smaller comp, but more interesting engineering for many candidates.

What is the take-home like?

Realistic and bounded — a couple hours of work. They pay for it. Quality of writing and reasoning matters more than completeness.
